preamble: The shift from the voiced bilabial stop [b] to the voiced labio-velar approximant [w] is a common form of lenition, particularly in intervocalic positions where the stop closure weakens into a glide.

examples.note: Intervocalic /b/ spirantizes to [β], which often approaches a glide [w] in rapid or relaxed speech.

Voiced stops after vowels became semivowels in Middle Persian: /b/ → /w/. Old Persian naiba- 'beautiful' became Middle Persian nēw. This lenition process began in the Arsacid period (before 3rd c. CE) and was complete in Sassanid Middle Persian.
Intervocalic /b/ → /w/; the form shows systematic b→w weakening in the postvocalic environment, characteristic of Middle Iranian lenition.

Eastern Iranian, including Pashto, shows widespread spirantization of voiced stops. Old Iranian /b/ is reflected as [w] in many Pashto forms, continuing the general Eastern Iranian tendency toward lenition of voiced stops in all positions.

sources: Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
Skjærvø, P. O. (2009). Middle Persian. In Windfuhr (ed.), The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
